---
title: "**Auburn Police Tactical Wellness Program**"
author: '*report generated by: Derek Anderson (dba0013@auburn.edu)*'
date: '*date: `r Sys.Date()`*'
output:
  pdf_document:
    toc: true
  md_document:
    variant: gfm
# this outputs the pdfs to the output folder instead of the main directory and outputs md and pdf simultaneously 
knit: (function(inputFile, encoding) {
  rmarkdown::render(inputFile, encoding = encoding, output_dir = "output", output_format = "all")
  })
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# set the default global option for displaying code chunks
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
# this sets the root file project to the main R project directory not location of current Rmd file
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = rprojroot::find_rstudio_root_file())
setwd(rprojroot::find_rstudio_root_file())

# load libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(tinytex) #helper package for LaTeX. Users may need full distribution such as MiKTeX for Windows/Linux or MacTeX for macOS.

# Read data in 
df <- read.csv("output/mock_data_graded.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

# verify that there is an output directory
if (!dir.exists("output")) dir.create("output")

# Modify this line to choose participant
p <- df[df$Participant_num == "PFS_001", ][1, ] # [1, ] is included to lock to 1 row in the RARE chance there is duplicate participant IDs

## Helper Functions

# Convert FMS movement score (0-3) to a descriptive performance label
fms_label <- function(score) {
  labels <- c(
    "0" = "Pain",
    "1" = "Poor Performance",
    "2" = "Sufficient Performance",
    "3" = "Excellent Performance"
  )
  unname(labels[as.character(score)])
}

# Convert FMS pain screen score (0/1) to a descriptive label
pain_label <- function(score) {
  labels <- c(
    "0" = "No Pain",
    "1" = "Pain"
  )
  unname(labels[as.character(score)])
}

# Calculate a value's percentile rank within a numeric vector
percentile_rank <- function(vec, val) {
  round(mean(vec <= val, na.rm = TRUE) * 100, 1)
}


```

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align='center', out.width='200px'}
knitr::include_graphics("input/logo.png")
```
\newpage

# Auburn Police Department Fitness Assessment

  Thank you for participating in the Auburn Police Department Fitness Assessment. During your visit, several aspects of your physical fitness were evaluated, including aerobic (cardiorespiratory) fitness, muscular strength, muscular endurance, lower-body power, flexibility, body composition, and balance. These measures provide an overall picture of your current physical readiness and general health.
  Your results will be compared to established normative fitness standards, which may account for factors such as age and sex, to help interpret how your performance compares to typical values seen in similar populations. In addition, you will receive information showing how your results compare to the overall performance of other members of the Auburn Police Department who participated in the assessment.
  
# Participant Identification and Confidentiality Statement

  The information contained in this report is associated only with Participant ID: **`r p$Participant_num`**. To protect personal privacy, individual names and other identifying information are not included in this document. All results are recorded and reported using only the assigned participant identification number.
  This report is intended solely for the individual participant associated with this identification number. The information presented reflects personal fitness and health assessment results and should be treated as confidential. Participants are encouraged to maintain control of this document and share it only at their own discretion.
  Any summaries produced from this program will present group-level results only. These summaries will not contain any information that could be used to identify individual participants.

\newpage

# Body Composition
```{r body-comp-table}
# Build a data frame with three side-by-side measurement/value pairs
body_comp_df <- data.frame(
  Measurement_1 = c(
    "Height (in)",
    "Weight (lbs)",
    "BMI",
    "BMI Category",
    ""
  ),
  Value_1 = c(
    as.character(p$height_in),
    as.character(p$total_mass_lbs),
    as.character(round(p$BMI, 1)),
    as.character(p$BMI_cat),
    ""
  ),
  Measurement_2 = c(
    "Body Fat (% of Body Weight)",
    "Body Fat % Category",
    "Fat Mass (lbs)",
    "Lean Muscle Mass (lbs)",
    "Lean Muscle Mass (% of Body Weight)"
  ),
  Value_2 = c(
    as.character(p$Body_Fat_perc),
    as.character(p$BFperc_cat),
    as.character(p$fat_mass_lbs),
    as.character(p$lean_muscle_mass_lbs),
    as.character(p$lean_muscle_mass_perc)
  ),
  Measurement_3 = c(
    "Bone Density (g/cm2)",
    "Bone Density (Age and Sex %ile)",
    "Bone Mineral Content (lbs)",
    "Resting Metabolic Rate (cal/day)",
    ""
  ),
  Value_3 = c(
    as.character(p$BMD_g_cm2),
    as.character(p$BMD_perc),
    as.character(p$BMC_lbs),
    as.character(p$RMR_cal_d),
    ""
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(
  body_comp_df,
  col.names = c("Measurement", "Value", "Measurement", "Value", "Measurement", "Value"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  caption   = "**Body Composition Summary Table**"
)
```

Your Body Mass Index is **`r round(p$BMI, 1)`**, placing you in the **`r p$BMI_cat`** category. Your body mass index is determined using your height and weight and does not take into account individual differences in muscle and fat mass. We assessed your body composition using dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA), which is considered the gold standard of assessment techniques. Your body fat percentage is **`r p$Body_Fat_perc`%**, and accounting for your age and sex, this places you in the **`r p$BFperc_cat`** category. Your bone mineral density percentile places you at the **`r p$BMD_perc`%ile** of people your age (note: this number is not the percentage of your total body mass that is bone mineral content).

Your resting metabolic rate is **`r p$RMR_cal_d`** calories per day. Note that this amount is only enough to support your baseline physiological needs and does not account for the energy required to perform physical activity. If you are trying to **lose weight**, you may need approximately **`r p$rmr_loss_low`** to **`r p$rmr_loss_high`** calories per day. If you are trying to **maintain your weight**, you may need approximately **`r p$rmr_maint_low`** to **`r p$rmr_maint_high`** calories per day. If you are trying to **gain weight or are extremely physically active**, you may need **`r p$rmr_gain`** calories per day or more.

\newpage

# Physical Performance

```{r perf-table}
perf_df <- data.frame(
  Test_1 = c(
    "Estimated VO2 Max (mL/kg/min)",
    "Grip Strength - Left (lbs)",
    "Grip Strength - Right (lbs)"
  ),
  Result_1 = c(
    as.character(p$VO2_max_est),
    as.character(p$grip_max_L_lbs),
    as.character(p$grip_max_R_lbs)
  ),
  Category_1 = c(
    as.character(p$VO2_cat),
    as.character(p$left_gs_cat),
    as.character(p$right_gs_cat)
  ),
  Test_2 = c(
    "Vertical Jump (in)",
    "Plank (mm:ss)",
    "Bench Press (reps)"
  ),
  Result_2 = c(
    as.character(p$vertical_jump_in),
    as.character(p$plank_mmss),
    as.character(p$bench_press_reps)
  ),
  Category_2 = c(
    as.character(p$VJ_cat),
    as.character(p$Plank_cat),
    as.character(p$BP_cat)
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(
  perf_df,
  col.names = c("Test", "Result", "Category", "Test", "Result", "Category"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  caption   = "**Physical Performance Summary Table**"
)
```

The results of your physical performance tests are listed above. Aerobic fitness is important for normal daily function, and reduced aerobic capacity is associated with premature death and disability. To evaluate your aerobic fitness, we used a graded exercise test (GXT), a standard protocol widely used in clinical and fitness settings. This protocol estimates your body's ability to deliver and utilize oxygen to working muscles, also known as your VO~2~Max. Your VO~2~Max of **`r p$VO2_max_est`** mL/kg/min for your age and sex places you in the **`r p$VO2_cat`** category.

We evaluated your muscular fitness using four exercises: the YMCA bench press test, handgrip strength test, plank test, and vertical jump test. The YMCA bench press test measures upper-body muscular strength and endurance, which reflects how well your chest, shoulders, and arms can repeatedly perform pushing movements. The handgrip strength test measures grip strength, which is associated with the strength of several major muscle groups in the upper body. Grip strength is also important for many everyday and occupational tasks such as lifting, carrying, and maintaining control during physical activities. The plank test measures the muscular endurance of the core muscles, including the abdominal and lower back muscles. These muscles help stabilize the body and support posture during movement. The vertical jump test measures lower-body power, which reflects your ability to generate force quickly using the muscles of the hips and legs. This type of power is important for movements such as sprinting, jumping, and quickly changing direction. Overall, these results, together with your estimated VO~2~Max, provide a snapshot of your current fitness level and help identify areas where additional training may be beneficial.

Your YMCA bench press test score of **`r p$bench_press_reps`** repetitions places you in the **`r p$BP_cat`** category for your age and sex. Your dominant-side grip strength was rated **`r p$GS_dom_cat`**, and your non-dominant side was rated **`r p$GS_nondom_cat`**. Your plank test score places the muscular endurance of your core muscles in the **`r p$Plank_cat`** category. Your vertical jump score of **`r p$vertical_jump_in`** inches places you in the **`r p$VJ_cat`** category for your sex and age.

\newpage

# Flexibility and Mobility

```{r flex-table}
flex_df <- data.frame(
  Assessment = c(
    "Sit and Reach (in)",
    "Functional Reach (in)",
    "FMS - Overhead Squat",
    "FMS - Modified Push-Up",
    "FMS Pain - Left Shoulder",
    "FMS Pain - Right Shoulder",
    "FMS Pain - Prone Press",
    "FMS Pain - Posterior Rock"
  ),
  Result = c(
    as.character(p$sit_reach_in),
    as.character(p$functional_reach_in),
    as.character(p$fms_overhead_squat),
    as.character(p$fms_modified_pushup),
    as.character(p$fms_pain_L_scap),
    as.character(p$fms_pain_R_scap),
    as.character(p$fms_pain_press_prone),
    as.character(p$fms_pain_posterior_rock)
  ),
  Category_Finding = c(
    as.character(p$SR_cat),
    as.character(p$FR_cat),
    fms_label(p$fms_overhead_squat),
    fms_label(p$fms_modified_pushup),
    pain_label(p$fms_pain_L_scap),
    pain_label(p$fms_pain_R_scap),
    pain_label(p$fms_pain_press_prone),
    pain_label(p$fms_pain_posterior_rock)
  ),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

knitr::kable(
  flex_df,
  col.names = c("Assessment", "Score", "Category / Finding"),
  booktabs  = TRUE,
  caption   = "**Flexibility and Mobility Results**"
)
```

We assessed your flexibility and mobility using the YMCA Sit-and-Reach test, the Functional Reach test, and several items from the Functional Movement Screening (FMS). Flexibility is important for maintaining good posture and balance, reducing your risk of falling when your balance is challenged, and preventing low-back pain.

Your sit-and-reach score of **`r p$sit_reach_in`** inches places you in the **`r p$SR_cat`** category for your age and sex. Your functional reach score of **`r p$functional_reach_in`** inches is classified as **`r p$FR_cat`**.

The FMS modified push-up was used to assess trunk stability. Your score of **`r p$fms_modified_pushup`** indicates **`r fms_label(p$fms_modified_pushup)`**. The FMS overhead squat was used to assess full-body kinetic function. Your score of **`r p$fms_overhead_squat`** indicates **`r fms_label(p$fms_overhead_squat)`**.

During the assessment, three pain-provocation screens were completed. The main focus of these screens is to identify heightened injury risk and key compensatory movement patterns associated with poor mobility and/or stability. Your screens indicated:

- **Shoulder Clearing Test:** `r pain_label(p$fms_pain_L_scap)` in the Left Shoulder; `r pain_label(p$fms_pain_R_scap)` in the Right Shoulder
- **Spinal Extension - Prone Press Up Test:** `r pain_label(p$fms_pain_press_prone)`
- **Spinal Flexion - Posterior Rock Test:** `r pain_label(p$fms_pain_posterior_rock)`

\newpage

# Comparison To the Force

The above tables represent how well you compare to individuals of similar age and or sex. The comparisons in this section will show how you compare to your peers and to the Auburn Police Force across a variety of key metrics.

The charts below represent department-wide distributions. The dashed blue line marks your value; the label shows your percentile rank among all participating officers.


```{r hist-function}
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
# Function: plot_dept_hist
#
# Plots a histogram of a department-wide variable and marks the
# individual participant's value with a dashed vertical line and
# their percentile rank as an annotation.
#
# Arguments:
#   dept_data  - data frame of all officers (used for the distribution)
#   var        - column name as a string (e.g., "BMI")
#   p_val      - participant's numeric value for that variable
#   x_label    - label for the x-axis
#   plot_title - title displayed above the plot
# -----------------------------------------------------------------------
plot_dept_hist <- function(dept_data, var, p_val, x_label, plot_title) {

  # Calculate the participant's percentile rank in the department
  pct <- percentile_rank(dept_data[[var]], p_val)

  ggplot(dept_data, aes(x = .data[[var]])) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 15, fill = "#D3D3D3", color = "white", alpha = 0.85) +
    geom_vline(xintercept  = p_val,
               color       = "#0072B2",
               linewidth   = 1.2,
               linetype    = "dashed") +
    annotate("text",
             x        = p_val,
             y        = Inf,
             label    = paste0(pct, "th %ile"),
             hjust    = -0.1,
             vjust    = 2,
             color    = "#0072B2",
             size     = 3,
             fontface = "bold") +
    labs(title = plot_title, x = x_label, y = "Count") +
    theme_classic() +
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"), plot.margin = margin(7.2, 36, 7.2, 7.2)) + # expands right margin to stop higher percentiles from being cut off
    coord_cartesian(clip = "off") # this was added to stop higher percentiles from being cut off
}
```

```{r comparison-plots, fig.height = 3.5, fig.width = 3.5, warning=FALSE}
# Build all nine histograms using the plot_dept_hist function
h1 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "BMI",
                     p$BMI,
                     "BMI",
                     "Body Mass Index (BMI)")

h2 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "Body_Fat_perc",
                     p$Body_Fat_perc,
                     "Body Fat (%)",
                     "Body Fat Percentage")

h3 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "lean_muscle_mass_lbs",
                     p$lean_muscle_mass_lbs,
                     "Lean Muscle Mass (lbs)",
                     "Lean Muscle Mass")

h4 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "VO2_max_est",
                     p$VO2_max_est,
                     "VO2 Max (mL/kg/min)",
                     "Estimated VO2 Max")

h5 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "grip_max_sum_lbs",
                     p$grip_max_sum_lbs,
                     "Combined Grip Strength (lbs)",
                     "Grip Strength (Sum of Both Hands)")

h6 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "bench_press_reps",
                     p$bench_press_reps,
                     "Bench Press (reps)",
                     "YMCA Bench Press Reps")

h7 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "vertical_jump_in",
                     p$vertical_jump_in,
                     "Vertical Jump (in)",
                     "Vertical Jump")

h8 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "plank_sec",
                     p$plank_sec,
                     "Plank Time (seconds)",
                     "Plank")

h9 <- plot_dept_hist(df, "sit_reach_in",
                     p$sit_reach_in,
                     "Sit and Reach (in)",
                     "Sit and Reach")

# Call the plots for display
h1
h2
h3
h4
h5
h6
h7
h8
h9
```
